[TITLE]: From School Blazers to Life Builders: Lessons from Alliance Girls High School Class of 1980
[METADATA/INTRO]: From School Blazers to Life Builders: Lessons from Alliance Girls High School Class of 1980 In 1975, we stepped onto the grounds of Alliance Girls High School (AGHS) as Form 1 students — young, hopeful, and full of possibility. Our...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: To the AGHS Class of 1980 — 50 years on — we honour the past, embrace the present, and remain committed to becoming. 
 Judy Omumbo PhD , Janice Muchai , Dr Sheila Macharia , Imelda Alegwa , Josephta O Mukobe , Elizabeth Karianjahe, Dr. Janet Macharia , Phoebe Kimani, Prof. BEATRICE KAGAI AMUGUNE , Muthoni Gathecha , Edith Botto , Nancy Ndirangu, Dr. Mercy Gichora , Dr. Nyambura Gichuhi, Prof. Sylvia Ojoo , Ngina Margaret Kairu , marianne ndegwa , Fatuma Abdalla, Dr. Joyce Wafula , Jane Kibwage

[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: The Game Changers: The Women of Sudan – Multi-Faith Neighbors Network
[METADATA/INTRO]: By Hurunnessa Fariad, Director of Outreach Multi-Faith Neighbors Network went to Sudan to facilitate a clergy retreat in Khartoum, Sudan. The retreat included Imams and pastors but what made this retreat historic is the inclusion of Muslim and Christian women!...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Our retreat started after lunch the next day. We expected 13 imams and 13 pastors and 20 Muslim and Christian women to participate. I was nervous, yet excited to meet the women. Being an extrovert helped me in my work but not speaking Arabic was making me feel a bit incomplete, even though we did have translators who were a great help. As the first few women walked in, I noticed that the majority were wearing headscarves and I assumed they were all Muslim. They wore beautiful vibrant colors, various textures, and styles, definitely expressing their connection to their Sudanese culture. I soon learned that even many Christian women wear the headscarf, which is normal in Sudanese culture. I observed their eyes wandering around the room, not knowing what to expect. Some women wore traditional Sudanese outfits, others wore western clothes, and some were a mix of both. One woman even wore a niqab (face covering).

[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: Resilient Women's Group — Mother Africa
[METADATA/INTRO]: Resilient Women’s Group In response to increased racism, xenophobia, and attacks on immigrants and refugees in our current political system, we created the Resilient Women’s group provide a support system and safety net to women in our communities. Women of...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: In response to increased racism, xenophobia, and attacks on immigrants and refugees in our current political system, we created the Resilient Women’s group provide a support system and safety net to women in our communities. Women of diverse backgrounds meet monthly to create sisterhood, overcome isolation, and address issues that matter to them and their families. This is offered through monthly Resilient Women meetings with speakers who provide information and resources on topics chosen by the group. 
 We are currently seeking funding to extend this valuable program into 2021. 
 Initial Funding partner: Seattle Foundation
